rationale_for_baseline_tier: Benign superficial bacterial folliculitis: follicle-centred pustules confined to a shaved/friction area, normal intervening skin, no systemic features, no confluent spreading erythema, no lymphangitis, and no palpable abscess. This is a self-care/primary-care condition. T2 (prompt GP or telehealth review within 24-48h) reflects the mild bacterial component, the anxious patient, and the value of obtaining a topical antibiotic and confirming it is not early cellulitis — but it is NOT urgent same-day, ED, or ambulance territory. The source note's 'urgent after-hours MBS / aggressive spreading staph' framing is billing/anxiety language, not true acuity.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Redness spreading into a large hot patch or coalescing between lesions (evolving cellulitis)
escalation_edges.rationale: Confluent spreading dermal erythema indicates cellulitis, which needs same-day in-person assessment and systemic antibiotics rather than topical therapy.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the redness starts spreading into a big hot patch or the spots join into one large red area, see a doctor in person the same day.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Fever, chills, rigors, or feeling generally unwell (systemic infection)
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same day — go to an emergency department if unwell
escalation_edges.rationale: Systemic features suggest a deeper or systemic infection that has outgrown topical management and needs urgent assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you develop a fever, chills, or start feeling really unwell in yourself, go to an emergency department the same day.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: A spot becomes a large, deep, firm, very painful lump (furuncle/abscess)
escalation_edges.rationale: A fluctuant collection may need incision and drainage — assessment that requires in-person palpation not possible via chat.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If any spot turns into a big, deep, very painful lump, see a doctor in person the same day as it may need to be drained.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Red streaks tracking up the leg (lymphangitis)
escalation_edges.rationale: Lymphangitic streaking indicates ascending infection needing urgent systemic antibiotics and assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you see red streaks running up your leg from the spots, go to an emergency department the same day.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Not settling after about 2 weeks, recurring, or similar spots appearing on household members
escalation_edges.rationale: Refractory or recurrent disease, or spread to contacts, warrants a routine GP review and consideration of a bacterial swab (including for resistant organisms).
escalation_edges.communication_template: If it hasn't settled in a couple of weeks, keeps coming back, or others in your household get similar spots, book a routine GP appointment for a swab.

contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Advise the patient to ask someone to check in on them and to lower their threshold to seek review if any spreading redness or fever develops, since no one else is present to notice a change.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This is a benign follicular irritation that you can largely manage at home: wash the area with an antibacterial wash twice daily, apply the prescribed ointment to the larger spots, throw away your razor, stop the heavy body butter, and wear loose cotton clothing. Arrange a GP or telehealth review in the next day or two to get the topical antibiotic and confirm it is settling. It is not an emergency and does not need hospital.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Your regular GP or a telehealth GP within 24-48 hours for the topical script; a pharmacist can supply the antibacterial wash immediately. After hours, call 13 HEALTH (13 43 25 84) for triage advice.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: After hours this can wait for the next day; only go to an emergency department or call for urgent help if the red-flag signs (spreading hot redness, red streaks, a large deep painful lump, or fever) develop.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Note when the eruption started, that it followed shaving plus tight clothing, and any change in the spots, to show the GP at review; a swab may be taken if it is refractory or recurrent.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Dismissing the presentation as pure self-care with NO safety-netting and no route to review — the one under-call that matters here, because an early cellulitis or an abscess needing palpation could be missed.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Patient is reassured, applies moisturiser, and is given no warning signs; an evolving cellulitis or abscess progresses over days before care is sought.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Sending a benign, afebrile, superficial folliculitis to the emergency department or calling an ambulance — unnecessary ED/000 resource use and reinforcement of the patient's disproportionate infection anxiety.
